title: The Science of Happiness
description: For questions 9 – 16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only one word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: The science of happiness, also known (0) as positive psychology, is a relatively new field that studies what makes life worth living. Rather than focusing solely on mental illness, positive psychology examines the factors that contribute to human flourishing and well-being.||Research has shown that happiness depends (9) on a combination of genetic factors, life circumstances, and intentional activities. Surprisingly, life circumstances like wealth and physical attractiveness have (10) less impact on long-term happiness than most people expect. Studies indicate that people generally return to their baseline happiness level after major life events, both positive and negative, (11) a phenomenon known as hedonic adaptation.||However, there are activities that can sustainably increase happiness. Regular exercise, maintaining strong social connections, and practicing gratitude (12) have all been proven to boost well-being. People who invest (13) in experiences rather than material possessions tend to be happier, as do those who help others through volunteering or charitable giving.||The concept of happiness varies (14) across cultures. In Western societies, happiness is often defined as feeling good and experiencing positive emotions. Eastern philosophies, (15) by contrast, may emphasise balance, acceptance, and finding meaning. Researchers now recognise that Researchers now recognise that sustainable happiness comes (16) not just from momentary pleasure but also from a sense of purpose and meaningful engagement with others.

example.text: The science of happiness, also known (0) positive psychology, is a relatively new field.

questions.explanation: Verb 'depends on' indicates dependence on several factors.
Comparativo: 'less impact than' compara grado de influencia.
Present perfect passive: 'have been proven' with a plural subject.
Part of the phrasal verb 'invest in', meaning to put resources or effort into something for future benefit.
Preposition 'across' indicates variation across different cultures.
Part of the fixed phrase 'by contrast', used to introduce a comparison that highlights a difference.
Part of the correlative expression 'not just... but also', used to emphasise that something includes more than one element.
